questions.question_text: How often must a Colorado broker renew their license?
questions.explanation: Colorado uses a triennial (3-year) renewal cycle. Brokers must complete 24 hours of continuing education during each cycle.

questions.question_text: How many hours of continuing education must a Colorado broker complete per renewal cycle?
questions.explanation: Colorado requires 24 hours of CE per 3-year renewal cycle. This includes three 4-hour Annual Commission Update (ACU) courses plus 12 hours of electives.

questions.question_text: A broker completes two ACU courses during a 3-year cycle but no other CE. How many more CE hours are needed?
questions.explanation: Two ACU courses = 8 hours. The broker needs 24 total, so 16 more hours are needed (one more 4-hour ACU + 12 hours of electives).

questions.question_text: The Annual Commission Update (ACU) course is how many hours?
questions.explanation: Each ACU is a 4-hour course. Three ACU courses (one per year) account for 12 of the 24 total CE hours required per triennial cycle.

questions.question_text: Starting in 2027, the deadline for completing the Annual Commission Update course changes to:
questions.explanation: Beginning in 2027, the ACU deadline shifts from December 31 to June 30 of each calendar year.

questions.question_text: A Colorado broker places their license on inactive status. What restriction applies?
distractors: They must return their license to DORA
They lose all accumulated CE credits
They must retake the licensing exam
questions.explanation: Inactive licensees cannot practice real estate. The license is not surrendered, and reactivation is possible by meeting CE and fee requirements.

questions.question_text: A broker's license expired two months ago because they failed to renew. To reinstate, the broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Complete additional CE and pay fees
distractors: Obtain a new employing broker first
questions.explanation: Reinstatement is available for up to three years after expiration — the broker completes any outstanding CE and pays escalating reinstatement fees. More than three years after expiration, the person must requalify, including retaking the exam.

questions.question_text: A broker on inactive status receives an offer to help a friend sell a house. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Decline until the license is reactivated
distractors: Proceed since it is a personal favor
Proceed if no commission is charged
questions.explanation: An inactive licensee cannot perform any licensed activity, even without compensation. The broker must reactivate the license first.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes an inactive license from a revoked license in Colorado?
distractors: Inactive requires a new exam to restore
Revoked licenses can be renewed annually
questions.explanation: An inactive license is a voluntary status that can be reactivated by meeting CE and fee requirements. Revocation is a disciplinary action that terminates the license.

questions.question_text: A broker who has been on inactive status for several years wants to reactivate. The broker will likely need to:
questions.correct_answer: Complete back CE hours and pay fees
distractors: Obtain a court order for reinstatement
questions.explanation: Reactivation after extended inactivity requires completing any missed CE hours during the inactive period plus paying applicable reinstatement fees.

questions.question_text: Which type of insurance is required for all active Colorado real estate licensees?
questions.explanation: Colorado requires all active licensees to maintain errors and omissions (E&O) insurance (C.R.S. 12-10-204). The Division of Real Estate contracts for a group E&O policy after a competitive bid, but licensees may instead obtain independent coverage meeting the Division's minimum requirements.

questions.question_text: A broker lets their E&O insurance policy lapse without obtaining new coverage. The likely consequence is:
distractors: A fine but continued active status
questions.explanation: Failure to maintain E&O insurance can result in the license being placed on inactive status. E&O coverage must be maintained continuously while the license is active.

questions.question_text: The Division of Real Estate makes a group E&O policy available to licensees. A broker who prefers a different insurer may:
distractors: Not opt out of the group policy
Only use the group policy provider
Use any policy regardless of coverage
questions.explanation: Licensees can enroll in the group E&O policy the Division contracts for, or obtain coverage independently — but an independent policy must comply with the Division's minimum coverage requirements.

questions.question_text: E&O insurance protects a Colorado broker against claims arising from:
distractors: Auto accidents while driving to listings
questions.explanation: Errors and omissions insurance covers claims of professional negligence, errors, or omissions in the broker's real estate practice — not general liability situations.

questions.question_text: A new licensee asks whether E&O insurance is optional in Colorado. The correct answer is:
questions.correct_answer: It is mandatory for all active licensees
distractors: It is required only for employing brokers
It is required only the first 3 years
questions.explanation: E&O insurance is mandatory for ALL active licensees in Colorado, not just employing brokers, and must be maintained continuously while the license is active. (Narrow exception: an attorney licensee whose legal-malpractice policy covers their brokerage activities satisfies the requirement — C.R.S. 12-10-204.)
